steps.title: Understand what an x-intercept means
steps.content:
An $x$-intercept is a point where the graph crosses the $x$-axis. At any such point, the $y$-coordinate is 0.

If $(a, 0)$ is an $x$-intercept of the graph of $y = f(x)$, then by definition $f(a) = 0$.

The problem tells us the graph has $x$-intercepts at $(-2, 0)$, $(3, 0)$, and $(10, 0)$. Therefore:
$$f(-2) = 0 \quad f(3) = 0 \quad f(10) = 0$$

steps.title: Match an x-intercept to the choices
steps.content:
Now evaluate each choice against what we know must be true:
  • Choice A — $f(0) = -2$: We have no information about the output at $x = 0$. Not necessarily true.
  • Choice B — $f(0) = 3$: Same issue. Not necessarily true.
  • Choice D — $f(2) = 0$: We do not have an $x$-intercept at $x = 2$. Not necessarily true.
  • Choice C — $f(10) = 0$: We are given that $(10, 0)$ is an $x$-intercept, so this must be true.
C is correct.
